disease.name: Neurocritical Care

emergencyPriority.notes: Neurologic emergencies require time-sensitive intervention to prevent secondary brain injury. The concept of 'time is brain' drives immediate imaging, ICP/CPP optimization, and seizure control within minutes of presentation.

mustNotMiss: Brain herniation syndromes: early recognition of Cushing triad (HTN, bradycardia, irregular respirations), anisocoria, posturing
Status epilepticus: continuous seizure > 5 min or two seizures without full recovery of consciousness; treat immediately with benzodiazepines
Cerebral vasospasm after aneurysmal SAH: peak incidence days 4-14; causes delayed cerebral ischemia
Ischemic stroke with large vessel occlusion: eligible for thrombectomy within 24 hours with advanced imaging
Malignant MCA syndrome with impending herniation requiring decompressive hemicraniectomy
Spinal cord compression: cauda equina syndrome, central cord syndrome, Brown-Sequard syndrome
diagnosticClues: ICP > 22 mmHg sustained = intracranial hypertension requiring treatment per BTF guidelines
CPP = MAP - ICP; target CPP 60-70 mmHg in TBI per BTF 4th edition
PbtO2 < 15-20 mmHg = brain tissue hypoxia, target > 20 mmHg
SEIZURE detection: abnormal rhythmic activity on EEG > 5 min; nonconvulsive SE requires continuous EEG for diagnosis
Herniation patterns: uncal (ipsilateral CN III palsy, contralateral hemiparesis), central (bilateral flexor then extensor posturing), tonsillar (respiratory arrest)
SAH vasospasm: transcranial doppler velocity > 200 cm/s or Lindegaard ratio > 6 suggests significant vasospasm
exclusionClues: Normal head CT does NOT exclude early ischemic stroke, small SAH, or cerebral venous sinus thrombosis
Normal CT angiogram effectively excludes large vessel occlusion but not small vessel or posterior circulation stroke
Negative EEG for > 30 min effectively excludes ongoing status epilepticus in a patient with normal mental status
Normal ICP does NOT exclude cerebral ischemia if CPP is low or PbtO2 is low
Improvement of symptoms with glucose administration in hypoglycemia rules out structural stroke

tools.name: Hunt-Hess Grade for SAH
classification: Grade 2: moderate-severe headache, nuchal rigidity, no deficit
Grade 3: drowsy, confusion, mild focal deficit
Grade 5: deep coma, decerebrate posturing, moribund

tools.name: Status Epilepticus Severity Score (STESS)
tools.interpretation: Score 0-2: good outcome; score 3-6: poor outcome. Guides aggressiveness of therapy.

historyFeatures: Onset: sudden vs progressive over minutes to hours vs waxing-waning
Witnessed vs unwitnessed: last known well time critical for stroke thrombolysis/thrombectomy
Headache: thunderclap (SAH, RCVS, cervical artery dissection, pituitary apoplexy, cerebral venous thrombosis)
Seizure activity: duration, semiology (focal vs generalized), prior epilepsy, post-ictal deficit (Todd paresis)
Trauma: mechanism, loss of consciousness, amnesia, anticoagulation/antiplatelet use
Comorbidities: HTN, DM, AFib, coagulopathy, stimulant use, pregnancy/postpartum, prior stroke/TIA
examFeatures: GCS: eye, verbal, motor components separately; FOUR score if intubated for more detail
Pupils: size, symmetry, reactivity to light (CN II, III). Anisocoria > 1-2 mm in dim light suggests CN III palsy from uncal herniation
Ocular motility: doll's eyes, caloric responses (brainstem function), nystagmus (cerebellar, vestibular, drug-induced)
Motor: pronator drift, hemiparesis, paraplegia, quadriplegia, posturing (decorticate vs decerebrate vs flaccid)
Reflexes: DTRs, Babinski sign, clonus, Hoffman sign
Cranial nerves: full CN exam, funduscopy for papilledema (ICP), retinal hemorrhage (child abuse/TBI)
riskFactors: Hypertension (primary risk for ICH, SAH, ischemic stroke)
Atrial fibrillation (ischemic stroke, particularly embolic pattern)
Anticoagulation (warfarin, DOACs: 5-10x increased ICH risk, worse outcomes)
Smoking, heavy alcohol use, stimulant use (cocaine, methamphetamine: HTN crisis, SAH, stroke)
Coagulopathy and inherited thrombophilia (hemorrhage and thrombosis)
Prior TBI, epilepsy, brain tumor, cerebral aneurysm, AV malformation

subtypes.name: Traumatic Brain Injury (TBI)
subtypes.pathophysiology: Primary injury from impact (contusion, hematoma, axonal shear) plus secondary injury cascades: excitotoxicity, free radical damage, neuroinflammation, cerebral edema, ischemia

subtypes.name: Aneurysmal Subarachnoid Hemorrhage
subtypes.pathophysiology: Rupture of saccular aneurysm at circle of Willis base causing subarachnoid blood, acute ICP spike, and subsequent vasospasm from blood breakdown products (oxyhemoglobin)

subtypes.name: Status Epilepticus
subtypes.pathophysiology: Failure of inhibitory GABA-mediated mechanisms and persistent excitatory NMDA/AMPA signaling leading to self-sustaining seizure activity with neuronal injury

subtypes.name: Intracerebral Hemorrhage (ICH)
subtypes.pathophysiology: Spontaneous rupture of small penetrating arteries damaged by hypertension or amyloid angiopathy causing hematoma expansion and peri-hematomal edema

mimics: Hypoglycemia: focal deficits, altered consciousness mimicking stroke; always check glucose first
Post-ictal state (Todd paresis) mimics stroke: resolving hemiparesis after seizure
Posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES): headache, seizure, visual changes, parieto-occipital white matter edema on MRI
Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis: headache, seizure, stroke-like symptoms; requires CT/MR venography
Reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome (RCVS): thunderclap headache, stuttering course, reversible vasospasm on CTA/MRA
Migraine with aura (hemiplegic migraine): aura may mimic stroke but gradual spread over 5-60 min, followed by headache

investigations.detail: First-line for all acute neurologic emergencies. Detects ICH, SAH, SDH, EDH, midline shift, hydrocephalus, large ischemic stroke (early signs: sulcal effacement, loss of grey-white differentiation, hyperdense MCA sign).

CTA: detects large vessel occlusion, cerebral aneurysm, cervical artery dissection. CT perfusion: identifies ischemic penumbra (irreversible core vs salvageable penumbra) for thrombectomy decision making up to 24 hours from last known well.

Indications: altered mental status after seizure, status epilepticus monitoring, post-arrest. Detects non-convulsive seizures/status epilepticus. Protocol: minimum 24-48 hours of monitoring. ACNS standardized critical care EEG terminology.

Non-invasive assessment of cerebral blood flow velocity in basal arteries. Used for: vasospasm surveillance after SAH, sickle cell disease screening, brain death confirmation. MCA velocity > 200 cm/s indicates severe vasospasm.

ICP monitor (external ventricular drain or intraparenchymal microsensor): gold standard ICP measurement, therapeutic CSF drainage. Brain tissue oxygen tension (PbtO2): measures local brain tissue oxygenation, target > 20 mmHg. Cerebral microdialysis: lactate/pyruvate ratio, glucose, glutamate, glycerol for metabolic distress. Jugular venous bulb oximetry (SjvO2): global cerebral oxygen extraction.

DWI: detects acute ischemia within minutes. SWI: detects microbleeds, hemosiderin, thrombus. MRA/MRV: circle of Willis, cervical vessels, venous sinuses. For: small strokes, TIA, cerebral venous thrombosis, encephalitis.

pathophysiology.summary: Secondary brain injury is the process by which initial neurologic insult is exacerbated by subsequent biochemical and physiologic cascades: excitotoxicity, calcium influx, oxidative stress, neuroinflammation, and spreading depolarizations. ICP/CPP dysregulation, impaired cerebral autoregulation, and loss of neurovascular coupling contribute to ongoing injury.
mechanisms: Excitotoxicity: excessive glutamate release activates NMDA/AMPA receptors causing massive neuronal calcium influx and cell death
Cerebral edema: cytotoxic (cellular swelling from energy failure) and vasogenic (BBB disruption, endothelial dysfunction)
Cerebral vasospasm: oxyhemoglobin-induced vasoconstriction, endothelin-1 upregulation, NO depletion, smooth muscle contraction
Spreading depolarizations: waves of depolarization in injured cortex causing metabolic crisis, worsen ischemic penumbra

complications: Herniation syndromes: uncal, central transtentorial, subfalcine, tonsillar; all require immediate intervention
Delayed cerebral ischemia from vasospasm after SAH (leading cause of poor outcome after initial bleed)
Ventilator-associated pneumonia and nosocomial infections in prolonged neuro-ICU stays
Venous thromboembolism (VTE): high risk in hemiplegic patients, but anticoagulation risk in ICH/SAH
Neurogenic stunned myocardium: catecholamine-induced myocardial injury after SAH, TBI, status epilepticus
Paroxysmal sympathetic hyperexcitability (PSH): episodic hypertension, tachycardia, diaphoresis, posturing in severe TBI
Brain death: irreversible loss of brainstem function with defined diagnostic criteria

management.detail: Tier 0: head elevation 30 degrees, midline, avoid PEEP > 10 cmH2O, maintain CPP 60-70. Tier 1: sedation (propofol or midazolam), CSF drainage via EVD. Tier 2: neuromuscular blockade, osmotherapy (mannitol 0.25-1g/kg or 3% hypertonic saline 30-60 mL bolus target Na 150-155), mild hyperventilation (PaCO2 32-35). Tier 3: decompressive hemicraniectomy, barbiturate coma (pentobarbital with burst suppression on EEG), therapeutic hypothermia (33-36C), or hyperventilation to PaCO2 30-32. ICP goal < 22 mmHg. Per BTF 4th edition and SCCM neurocritical care guidelines.

Hyperventilate immediately (PaCO2 25-30) as BRIDGE to definitive therapy. Bolus mannitol 1 g/kg IV or 3% hypertonic saline 30-60 mL IV push. Elevate head of bed. Reverse any sedatives (naloxone, flumazenil if overdose suspected). Emergent neurosurgery consultation. Consider emergent craniotomy, EVD placement for CSF diversion, or decompressive hemicraniectomy. Code Neuro call for system activation.

Stabilization (0-5 min): ABCs, glucose check, thiamine 100 mg IV, lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg IV or midazolam 10 mg IM. First-line (5-20 min): fosphenytoin 20 mg PE/kg IV or valproate 40 mg/kg IV. Refractory (20-40 min): continuous midazolam 0.2 mg/kg IV load then 0.05-2 mg/kg/hr or propofol 1-2 mg/kg load then 30-200 ug/kg/min. Super-refractory (> 24h): IV ketamine, pentobarbital coma, pyridoxine (if deficiency suspected), immunomodulation (if autoimmune), or ketogenic diet. Continuous EEG monitoring throughout. Per NCS/SCCM guidelines.

Prophylaxis: oral nimodipine 60 mg q4h x 21 days (improves outcome even without preventing vasospasm). Hemodynamic augmentation: goal MAP > 90, target CPP > 70, euvolemia (NOT triple-H therapy as previously recommended; induced HTN alone now preferred per neurocritical care consensus). Vasospasm detection: daily TCD, CTA if TCD or clinical change. Treatment: endovascular therapy (intra-arterial verapamil/nicardipine, angioplasty). Rescue: induced HTN (norepinephrine) to MAP 90-120. Vasospasm peaks days 4-14. Per Neurocritical Care Society guidelines.

Target core temperature 36-37C for fever prevention (fever worsens secondary brain injury). Avoid prophylactic hypothermia (POLAR trial, Eurotherm3235 trial showed no benefit, possible harm). For refractory intracranial hypertension: consider hypothermia to 33-36C (tier 3 therapy) with slow rewarming < 0.5C per hour. Fever: treat aggressively with surface cooling, cold IV fluids, antipyretics. Maintain normothermia per BTF 4th edition.

ICP: target < 22 mmHg. CPP: target 60-70 mmHg (BTF 4th). PbtO2: target > 20 mmHg. Use Licox or Neurovent sensor in penumbral brain tissue. SjvO2: target 55-75%. Cerebral microdialysis: LPR > 40 suggests metabolic crisis. Autoregulation monitoring: PRx (pressure reactivity index) goal < 0.3. Integrate all modalities to individualize therapy. Example: low PbtO2 with normal ICP = optimize CPP and Hb, avoid hyperventilation. Per consensus multimodal monitoring guidelines.

Indications: malignant MCA infarction with midline shift and clinical deterioration (within 48 hours, age < 60, per DECIMAL/DESTINY/HAMLET trials). Also: refractory ICP after TBI (RESCUEicp trial showed improved survival but more vegetative survivors). Also: cerebellar ICH/infarction with brainstem compression (ventriculostomy + suboccipital decompression). Timing is critical before irreversible brainstem damage.
